serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mustard powder is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 3 g), used as a spice or flavouring agent in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Mustard seed, ground — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02046)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). HPB values used as primary; USDA SR Legacy used for secondary nutrients not reported by HPB (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, water, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium). Minor rounding differences noted; HPB values retained for core macros.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices and Condiments — Analogous estimate based on mustard seed GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mustard powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on mustard seed GI data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the food's low net carbohydrate content (17.2g/100g total carbohydrate minus 5.0g fibre = 12.2g net carbohydrate). Glycemic load per 3g serving = (35 × 0.37g net carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.13, rounded to 0.2. Practical glycaemic impact is negligible given typical spice-level usage.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g) and very high potassium (650 mg/100g) — favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Mustard powder is an excellent low-sodium, high-potassium spice for hypertensive individuals; use as a dry spice rather than prepared mustard condiments, which contain added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), moderate fibre (5g/100g), and glucosinolate compounds with potential insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mustard powder has a low glycaemic index and negligible glycaemic load at culinary serving sizes; glucosinolates in mustard seed may support insulin sensitivity, making it a suitable spice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High PUFA content (predominantly omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid), very low saturated fat (~2.1g/100g), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mustard powder provides a favourable fatty acid profile dominated by omega-3 ALA and monounsaturated fats, with negligible saturated fat and no cholesterol; at culinary serving sizes it poses no cardiovascular risk and may confer anti-inflammatory benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 ALA, glucosinolates, and selenium — all with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mustard powder's high selenium content (208 mcg/100g) and omega-3 ALA profile support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; use in culinary quantities only, as large amounts may cause gastrointestinal irritation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mustard powder is a finely ground spice produced by milling dried mustard seeds (Brassica juncea or Brassica hirta/Sinapis alba) into a pale yellow powder. It has a sharp, pungent, and intensely aromatic flavour that becomes more pronounced when hydrated with water or vinegar, activating the enzyme myrosinase to release allyl isothiocyanate — the compound responsible for its characteristic heat. Nutritionally, mustard powder is a calorie-dense spice (547 kcal/100g) with a high protein content (29.4g/100g), substantial fat (39.5g/100g, predominantly omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid and monounsaturated fats), moderate carbohydrates (17.2g/100g), and meaningful dietary fibre (5.0g/100g). It is exceptionally rich in selenium (208 mcg/100g), phosphorus (828 mg/100g), magnesium (238 mg/100g), potassium (650 mg/100g), and folate (162 mcg DFE/100g). Sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100g, making it an ideal seasoning for sodium-restricted diets. Mustard powder is used globally as a spice in curries, marinades, salad dressings, pickles, and dry rubs. It is a staple in British, American, Indian, and European cuisines. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals with hypertension (very low sodium, high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, fibre, glucosinolates), and cardiovascular disease (favourable fatty acid profile, zero cholesterol). It is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Mustard is a declared allergen in many jurisdictions and must be labelled accordingly.
common_user_queries: Is mustard powder high in protein?
What is the glycemic index of mustard powder?
What nutrients are in mustard powder?
Is mustard powder the same as dry mustard?
How many calories in mustard powder?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database at 0.95 confidence (reviewed, canonical record). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 170931 — mustard seed, ground), an approved primary source. Values are consistent between both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct GI measurement exists for mustard powder.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed). 2. Secondary nutrients (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water_g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — mustard seed, ground (NDB 170931). 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for mustard seed, ground. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: naturally zero — mustard powder is a plant-derived food containing no vitamin B12. 5. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: naturally zero — mustard powder is a plant-derived food with no meaningful vitamin D content per USDA SR Legacy. 6. GI of approximately 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mustard powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) mustard seed data and net carbohydrate content. 7. Mustard is a declared allergen (EU, UK, Canada) and must be labelled accordingly. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches; FoodEx2 code A0CKP cross-referenced against EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue for ground mustard seeds.
